draft.introduction: Jesus instructs his disciples not to fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. The passage establishes the priority of the soul over the body and grounds courage in divine providence.

translation.text: And do not fear those who kill the body but cannot kill the soul. Rather fear him who can destroy both soul and body in hell. Are not two sparrows sold for a penny? And not one of them will fall to the ground apart from your Father. But even the hairs of your head are all numbered. Fear not, therefore; you are of more value than many sparrows.
translation.original_text: καὶ μὴ φοβεῖσθε ἀπὸ τῶν ἀποκτεννόντων τὸ σῶμα

translator_notes.note: The distinction between killing the body and destroying the soul (ψυχή) implies a body-soul anthropology often associated with Greek philosophy. Some scholars see here the influence of Hellenistic thought on Jesus’ teaching; others argue it reflects Jewish apocalyptic views of post-mortem existence.
